questionText: A tracking point moves along the path $x = 4\cos t$, $y = 3\sin t$, for $0 \le t \le \pi$, with $x$ and $y$ in metres. (a) Find $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ in terms of $t$. (b) Find the value(s) of $t$ in the given interval for which the tangent to the path is parallel to the line $3x + 4y = 12$, rejecting any that are out of range. (c) Find the equation of the tangent at that point.

steps.description: Differentiate x with respect to t
steps.explanation: The horizontal velocity comes from differentiating $4\cos t$, giving $-4\sin t$.

steps.description: Differentiate y with respect to t
steps.explanation: Differentiating $3\sin t$ gives the vertical velocity $3\cos t$.

steps.description: Form the gradient of the path
steps.explanation: Dividing the velocity components gives the slope of the path at parameter $t$, valid where $\sin t \neq 0$.

steps.description: Find the gradient of the given line
steps.explanation: Two lines are parallel when their gradients match, so we rearrange the given line to read off its gradient $-\tfrac{3}{4}$.

steps.description: Set the path gradient equal to the line's gradient
steps.explanation: For the tangent to be parallel to the line, the gradient of the path must equal $-\tfrac{3}{4}$.
Removing the common factor $-\tfrac{3}{4}$ from both sides leaves a simple ratio of $\cos t$ to $\sin t$.
The ratio $\tfrac{\cos t}{\sin t}$ is $\cot t$; setting it to $1$ is the same as $\tan t = 1$.
The solutions of $\tan t = 1$ repeat every $\pi$; the first two are $\tfrac{\pi}{4}$ and $\tfrac{5\pi}{4}$.

steps.description: Apply the interval and reject out-of-range solutions
steps.explanation: Only $t = \tfrac{\pi}{4}$ lies in the modelled interval; $\tfrac{5\pi}{4}$ is outside $0 \le t \le \pi$, so it is rejected.

steps.description: Confirm the tangent is defined there
steps.explanation: The gradient formula needs $\dot{x}\neq 0$; since $\sin\tfrac{\pi}{4}\neq 0$ the tangent is well defined at this point.
Substituting $t = \tfrac{\pi}{4}$ into $x = 4\cos t$ gives the horizontal coordinate of the point.
Substituting the same time into $y = 3\sin t$ gives the vertical coordinate, so the point is $\left(2\sqrt{2},\,\tfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{2}\right)$.

steps.description: Write the tangent in point-gradient form
steps.explanation: Using the point and the known gradient $-\tfrac{3}{4}$ builds the tangent line.

Expanding gives $-\tfrac{3}{4}\times(-2\sqrt{2}) = \tfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{2}$, which adds to the existing constant to give $3\sqrt{2}$; the intercept differs from the given line's, confirming the tangent is parallel, not identical.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: (a) $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = -\dfrac{3\cos t}{4\sin t}$. (b) The tangent is parallel to $3x + 4y = 12$ at $t = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$ ($t = \tfrac{5\pi}{4}$ is rejected as out of range). (c) The tangent there is $y = -\dfrac{3}{4}x + 3\sqrt{2}$.
